verses.text: And there happened to be there a man of Belial, whose name was Sheba, the son of Bichri, a Benjamite: and he blew a trumpet, and said, We have no part in David, neither have we inheritance in the son of Jesse: every man to his tents, O Israel. So every man of Israel went up from after David, and followed Sheba the son of Bichri: but the men of Judah clave unto their king, from Jordan even to Jerusalem. Guidance: The inter-tribal quarrel at the end of chapter 19 explodes into a second rebellion. Sheba — described as 'a man of Belial' (a worthless man, the recurring designation for political agitators in the Old Testament) — son of Bichri, a Benjamite (Saul's tribe; the loyalist faction of Saulides has been waiting for an excuse). He blows a trumpet — the standard signal for tribal mobilization — and delivers the most dangerous political slogan of the early monarchy: 'We have no part in David, neither have we inheritance in the son of Jesse: every man to his tents, O Israel.' The phrase 'we have no part in David' will be repeated word for word at the kingdom's actual split under Rehoboam (1 Kgs 12:16). Sheba is, in effect, the prototype of the northern separatist movement. He calls the country home — 'every man to his tents' — i.e., desert the king. The slogan works on the immediate tribal grievance from chapter 19. 'Every man of Israel went up from after David' — the ten northern tribes desert. 'But the men of Judah clave unto their king' — Judah stays. The unified kingdom has, in a single trumpet blast, fractured into the Judah-Israel split that will become permanent four reigns later. The chapter is showing us how short the restoration's peace was: less than a day after crossing the Jordan, the country is splitting again.

And David came to his house at Jerusalem; and the king took the ten women his concubines, whom he had left to keep the house, and put them in ward, and fed them, but went not in unto them. So they were shut up unto the day of their death, living in widowhood. Guidance: David reaches Jerusalem. His first domestic act is heartbreaking and unavoidable. The ten concubines Absalom had publicly taken (16:21-22) cannot, by Israelite law and royal custom, be received back as wives. (The standard view: a king cannot maintain marital relations with a woman who has been publicly given to another king, particularly his own son — the political and ritual implications would compromise both his and the kingdom's standing.) David puts them under protected confinement: 'in ward' (a guarded household), fed and provided for, but never visited as wives. 'So they were shut up unto the day of their death, living in widowhood.' The Hebrew almnut chayyut — 'living-widowhood' — is one of the most heartbreaking phrases in the Old Testament. The ten women live the rest of their lives as women-with-no-husband, husband-still-living. They are the casualties Nathan's prophecy (12:11) named in advance and Absalom's vanity in chapter 16 produced. David provides for them but cannot restore them. The chapter is showing us the unhealable victims of his original sin. Bathsheba lived to be queen mother of Solomon; the ten concubines lived to die unmarried under royal guard. The chapter does not gloss the cost.

Then said the king to Amasa, Assemble me the men of Judah within three days, and be thou here present. So Amasa went to assemble the men of Judah: but he tarried longer than the set time which he had appointed him. Guidance: David activates his new army commander. He gave Amasa Joab's job back in 19:13; now he gives him the first major assignment. 'Assemble me the men of Judah within three days, and be thou here present.' A three-day muster — tight but achievable. Amasa fails the deadline. 'He tarried longer than the set time.' We are not told why. Possibly Amasa, recently the rebel commander, has not yet rebuilt his networks of trust with Judahite leaders; possibly the political resentment over his appointment is slowing the response. Either way, the failure to muster on schedule is going to cost him his life. Joab — the demoted commander — is going to interpret Amasa's delay as proof of incompetence and use the moment to reclaim his position by removing his rival. The chapter is showing us the cost of David's political decision to elevate Amasa. The unification gesture was wise; the operational consequence was that David did not have a competent commander ready to chase Sheba.

And David said to Abishai, Now shall Sheba the son of Bichri do us more harm than did Absalom: take thou thy lord's servants, and pursue after him, lest he get him fenced cities, and escape us. And there went out after him Joab's men, and the Cherethites, and the Pelethites, and all the mighty men: and they went out of Jerusalem, to pursue after Sheba the son of Bichri. Guidance: David bypasses both Amasa (late) and Joab (demoted, not on speaking terms) and turns to Abishai (Joab's brother, second-in-command). 'Now shall Sheba do us more harm than did Absalom: take thou thy lord's servants, and pursue after him, lest he get him fenced cities, and escape us.' David's analysis: Sheba is a guerrilla; if he reaches fortified cities, the rebellion becomes a siege war. Strike fast. Abishai mobilizes — but note who actually goes: 'Joab's men, and the Cherethites, and the Pelethites, and all the mighty men.' Joab's professional corps marches under Abishai's nominal command, and Joab quietly accompanies. The chapter is showing us that even demoted Joab is operationally indispensable. David named Abishai, but Joab is the one heading out with the army. The stage is set for Joab to encounter Amasa on the road and resolve the command question by murder.

When they were at the great stone which is in Gibeon, Amasa went before them. And Joab's garment that he had put on was girded unto him, and upon it a girdle with a sword fastened upon his loins in the sheath thereof; and as he went forth it fell out. And Joab said to Amasa, Art thou in health, my brother? And Joab took Amasa by the beard with the right hand to kiss him. But Amasa took no heed to the sword that was in Joab's hand: so he smote him therewith in the fifth rib, and shed out his bowels to the ground, and struck him not again; and he died. So Joab and Abishai his brother pursued after Sheba the son of Bichri. Guidance: At Gibeon's 'great stone' (a known landmark), Joab's army meets Amasa. The narrator details the costume: Joab is in military dress with a sheathed sword on his belt — and the sword 'fell out' as he walked forward. The detail is the chapter's key piece of evidence. Joab has rigged his scabbard so the sword will drop into his left hand when he steps forward — a planned move that lets him hold the weapon naturally while pretending he had no plan to draw it. He approaches Amasa with the casual question of a brother officer: 'Art thou in health, my brother?' He grasps Amasa's beard with his right hand to give the standard kiss of greeting. Amasa, expecting the kiss, lets his guard down. Joab, in the same motion, drives the sword in his left hand into Amasa's abdomen — 'in the fifth rib' (the chapter's recurring kill-phrase — Asahel, Abner, Ish-bosheth, now Amasa). 'Shed out his bowels to the ground, and struck him not again; and he died.' One thrust kills him. The murder is the same template as Joab's killing of Abner in 3:27 — pretend embrace, hidden blade, sudden wound. The chapter is showing us a Joab who has now murdered two senior commanders by the same trick. Amasa, his cousin, did not learn from Abner's death — or trusted Joab too much to imagine a repeat. The narrator does not editorialize; he just records what happened. David will eventually charge Solomon (1 Kgs 2:5-6) to settle Joab's accounts for both murders.

And one of Joab's men stood by him, and said, He that favoureth Joab, and he that is for David, let him go after Joab. And Amasa wallowed in blood in the midst of the highway. And when the man saw that all the people stood still, he removed Amasa out of the highway into the field, and cast a cloth upon him, when he saw that every one that came by him stood still. When he was removed out of the highway, all the people went on after Joab, to pursue after Sheba the son of Bichri. Guidance: A junior officer in Joab's command tries to restart the march. 'He that favoureth Joab, and he that is for David, let him go after Joab.' He couples Joab's name with David's, framing the murder as an act compatible with loyalty to the king (it was not, but the soldier needs the troops moving). The problem: 'Amasa wallowed in blood in the midst of the highway.' The body is in the middle of the road. Soldiers passing by stop, see the gore, and freeze. The body has become a checkpoint. The officer realizes he must remove the visual obstacle. He drags Amasa off the road into the field and throws a cloth over him. Once the body is out of sight, the troops move on. The chapter is showing us the practical aftermath of an in-camp murder: blood in the road stops the army; covering the body resumes the march. The detail is brutal and realistic. The army is following Joab now — Joab, the very man who murdered the king's appointed commander. The chapter has just installed Joab back in his old position by force.

And he went through all the tribes of Israel unto Abel, and to Beth-maachah, and all the Berites: and they were gathered together, and went also after him. And they came and besieged him in Abel of Beth-maachah, and they cast up a bank against the city, and it stood in the trench: and all the people that were with Joab battered the wall, to throw it down. Guidance: Sheba flees north, hopping across the northern tribes, finally taking refuge in 'Abel of Beth-maachah' — a fortified town in the far north of Israel, near Dan, at the very edge of the country. The Berites (probably the men of Beeroth from chapter 4, or a tribal name for northern affiliates) gather to him; he has a residual support base. Joab's army arrives at Abel and lays siege. They build a siege-ramp ('cast up a bank against the city') and begin to 'batter the wall, to throw it down.' Standard ancient siege procedure: pile up earthworks to climb over walls and undermine the foundations. Abel is going to be destroyed — not just Sheba inside it, but the whole northern town that gave him refuge. The chapter is showing us the disproportion: one rebel hides in a city; the city about to be destroyed. The wise woman is about to intervene.

Then cried a wise woman out of the city, Hear, hear; say, I pray you, unto Joab, Come near hither, that I may speak with thee. And when he was come near unto her, the woman said, Art thou Joab? And he answered, I am he. Then she said unto him, Hear the words of thine handmaid. And he answered, I do hear. Then she spake, saying, They were wont to speak in old time, saying, They shall surely ask counsel at Abel: and so they ended the matter. I am one of them that are peaceable and faithful in Israel: thou seekest to destroy a city and a mother in Israel: why wilt thou swallow up the inheritance of the LORD? Guidance: A 'wise woman' from inside the city calls out. The chapter does not give us her name (like the wise woman of Tekoa in chapter 14, anonymous in the text). She calls Joab to the wall. 'Art thou Joab?' Confirming the commander. 'Hear the words of thine handmaid.' He agrees to listen. Then her speech, one of the most beautiful in 2 Samuel. (1) 'They were wont to speak in old time, saying, They shall surely ask counsel at Abel.' She invokes Abel's ancient reputation as a wisdom-center; a proverb of the period held that disputes should be resolved by 'asking counsel at Abel.' Abel is a city of wisdom, not of rebellion. (2) 'I am one of them that are peaceable and faithful in Israel.' She identifies herself with the wisdom-tradition of the city. (3) 'Thou seekest to destroy a city and a mother in Israel.' She names the disproportion. 'A mother in Israel' is the Hebrew idiom for a town with regional cultural significance — a place that nurtures wisdom-tradition for surrounding districts. Destroying Abel would be like destroying a university; the loss is cultural, not just demographic. (4) 'Why wilt thou swallow up the inheritance of the LORD?' The theological framing: the town is the LORD's possession. The chapter is showing us a woman speaking from a wall to the commander of an invading army, framing the negotiation in terms of wisdom-heritage, motherhood, and the LORD's inheritance. The argument is going to work.

And Joab answered and said, Far be it, far be it from me, that I should swallow up or destroy. The matter is not so: but a man of mount Ephraim, Sheba the son of Bichri by name, hath lifted up his hand against the king, even against David: deliver him only, and I will depart from the city. And the woman said unto Joab, Behold, his head shall be thrown to thee over the wall. Then the woman went unto all the people in her wisdom. And they cut off the head of Sheba the son of Bichri, and cast it out to Joab. And he blew a trumpet, and they retired from the city, every man to his tent. And Joab returned to Jerusalem unto the king. Guidance: Joab agrees. 'Far be it, far be it from me, that I should swallow up or destroy.' The protest is rote, but the willingness to negotiate is sincere. 'A man of mount Ephraim, Sheba the son of Bichri… hath lifted up his hand against the king.' The argument: I am after one rebel, not the city. 'Deliver him only, and I will depart from the city.' Standard ancient siege resolution: extradite the fugitive, save the town. The woman accepts the deal: 'Behold, his head shall be thrown to thee over the wall.' She does not consult with the city elders or the council; she goes 'unto all the people in her wisdom' — addressing the city herself. The people of Abel kill Sheba and throw his head over the wall. Joab sounds the recall trumpet; the army disperses; Joab returns to Jerusalem. The chapter is showing us a single woman ending a war that David's two top commanders had not been able to prevent. Her name is not recorded; her wisdom is. There is a category of historical figure who saves a city from destruction with a single speech, never to be named in the lineage of kings, but remembered in the recurring biblical motif of 'wise women' (Tekoa, Abel) whose words turn the violence of the moment toward a less destructive outcome. The chapter is teaching us that wise women sometimes accomplish what generals cannot. The rebellion of Sheba is over.

And Joab answered and said, Far be it, far be it from me, that I should swallow up or destroy. The matter is not so: but a man of mount Ephraim, Sheba the son of Bichri by name, hath lifted up his hand against the king, even against David: deliver him only, and I will depart from the city. And the woman said unto Joab, Behold, his head shall be thrown to thee over the wall. Then the woman went unto all the people in her wisdom. And they cut off the head of Sheba the son of Bichri, and cast it out to Joab. And he blew a trumpet, and they retired from the city, every man to his tent. And Joab returned to Jerusalem unto the king. Guidance: Joab agrees. 'Far be it, far be it from me, that I should swallow up or destroy.' The argument: I am after one rebel, not the city. 'Deliver him only, and I will depart from the city.' Standard ancient siege resolution: extradite the fugitive, save the town. The woman accepts the deal: 'Behold, his head shall be thrown to thee over the wall.' She does not consult with the city elders or the council; she goes 'unto all the people in her wisdom' — addressing the city herself. The people of Abel kill Sheba and throw his head over the wall. Joab sounds the recall trumpet; the army disperses; Joab returns to Jerusalem. The chapter is showing us a single woman ending a war that David's two top commanders had not been able to prevent. Her name is not recorded; her wisdom is. There is a category of historical figure who saves a city from destruction with a single speech, never to be named in the lineage of kings, but remembered in the recurring biblical motif of 'wise women' (Tekoa, Abel) whose words turn the violence of the moment toward a less destructive outcome. The chapter is teaching us that wise women sometimes accomplish what generals cannot. The rebellion of Sheba is over.

The chapter is teaching us that wise women sometimes accomplish what generals cannot. The rebellion of Sheba is over.

Now Joab was over all the host of Israel: and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada was over the Cherethites and over the Pelethites: And Adoram was over the tribute: and Jehoshaphat the son of Ahilud was recorder: And Sheva was scribe: and Zadok and Abiathar were the priests: And Ira also the Jairite was a chief ruler about David. Guidance: A cabinet roster — the second in 2 Samuel after 8:16-18. The composition has shifted to reflect the post-rebellion realities. (1) Joab is back 'over all the host of Israel' — the murder of Amasa has restored him to command. David has not punished him; the political reality is that Joab is too dangerous to remove and too useful to lose. (2) Benaiah son of Jehoiada — still over the Cherethites and Pelethites (the foreign bodyguard) as in 8:18. Benaiah will eventually replace Joab under Solomon (1 Kgs 2:35). (3) Adoram — a new addition, 'over the tribute' (mas — forced labor / taxation). This is a new institutional position; David is formalizing the labor and tax administration for the first time. The same Adoram (or his son with the same title) will be stoned to death by the northern tribes at Rehoboam's accession in 1 Kgs 12:18 — labor-conscription was unpopular. (4) Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud — recorder, the senior bureaucratic chief, same role as 8:16. (5) Sheva — scribe (chief secretary), the position formerly held by Seraiah in 8:17. The name change suggests a personnel turnover. (6) Zadok and Abiathar — priests, same as 8:17. (7) Ira the Jairite — a new official, 'chief ruler' (kohen, the same word as 'priest,' here probably 'chief minister'). Ira is otherwise unknown; he is part of the post-rebellion restructuring. The chapter is showing us a David who, after the trauma of the rebellions, is administering a more institutionalized kingdom: tribute/labor, scribal continuity, priestly continuity, and added personal advisers. The chapter ends with the kingdom restored, the rebellions ended, the apparatus of state staffed. The next chapter (21) will jump forward several years to a famine and a Saulide-revenge episode. The Davidic monarchy, scarred and reorganized, will continue for another decade or so before the final illness narratives begin.
